In Luke 7, Jesus encounters three responses to His authority, and each one reveals something you need to see about faith, worth, and love. A Roman centurion—a man of genuine power—approaches Jesus with a humility that actually moves Christ to marvel. This military officer understands something profound: that authority works by obedience, and Jesus exercises authority over sickness and death the way a centurion commands soldiers. He doesn't need physical proximity to heal; He simply needs to speak. What astounds Jesus is not the centurion's need, but the centurion's faith in Jesus's power, coming from a man who grasped his own unworthiness yet dared to believe anyway. Notice how the religious leaders defended this pagan's character while he himself insisted he didn't deserve Jesus's presence—yet he asked anyway. This is faith that knows its place before God while also knowing that God's power transcends human categories of merit.

Immediately after, Jesus approaches a funeral procession in Nain and sees a widow about to lose her only son. There's no request here, no demonstration of faith. Jesus's compassion moves Him first, and in that moment He speaks life back into a dead body. The crowd recognizes something unprecedented: "God has visited His people." This is not just a miracle. This is the presence of God in human form, defeating death itself. The resurrection of the dead was always a sign reserved for the end of all things, and here it happens at the word of a man. Yet John the Baptist, languishing in prison, begins to doubt. He sends messengers asking, "Are You the One who was to come?" Jesus doesn't rebuke his doubt. Instead, He points to what His works declare: the blind see, the dead are raised, and good news comes to the poor. These are the signs of God's kingdom breaking into a broken world.

But the final encounter—with the sinful woman and the Pharisee Simon—shows you the deeper issue beneath all the others. Simon looks at Jesus and calculates worth: this woman is a sinner; therefore Jesus couldn't be a prophet. But Jesus sees Simon and asks which debtor loves more: the one forgiven fifty denarii or the one forgiven five hundred? Simon's arithmetic fails him. He cannot measure love or worth the way God does. The woman, ashamed and weeping, acts out what she understands: she has been forgiven much, and therefore she loves much. She doesn't question whether she deserves to be there. She knows she's unworthy, yet she encounters the One whose forgiveness remakes her worth. Jesus's final word to her—"Your faith has saved you"—echoes the centurion's story. Faith is not about being worthy. It's about believing that God's power and mercy transcend what you deserve. The question Luke leaves you with is this: Do you love much, or little? That depends entirely on whether you grasp how much you've actually been forgiven.
